subject: Physics
q:
Three students S1, S2 and S3 perform an experiment for determining the acceleration due to gravity (g) using a simple pendulum. They use different lengths of pendulum and record time for different number of oscillations. The observations are as shown in the table.

Student No. Length of pendulum
(cm)		 Number of oscillations
(n)		 Total time for
n oscillations		 Time
period
(s)
1. 64.0 8 128.0 16.0
2. 64.0 4 64.0 16.0
3. 20.0 4 36.0 9.0

(Least count of length =0.1 m, least count for time =0.1 s)

If E1, E2 and E3 are the percentage errors in g for students 1, 2 and 3, respectively, then the minimum percentage error is obtained by student no          .
question:
Three students S1, S2 and S3 perform an experiment for determining the acceleration due to gravity (g) using a simple pendulum. They use different lengths of pendulum and record time for different number of oscillations. The observations are as shown in the table.

Student No. Length of pendulum
(cm)		 Number of oscillations
(n)		 Total time for
n oscillations		 Time
period
(s)
1. 64.0 8 128.0 16.0
2. 64.0 4 64.0 16.0
3. 20.0 4 36.0 9.0

(Least count of length =0.1 m, least count for time =0.1 s)

If E1, E2 and E3 are the percentage errors in g for students 1, 2 and 3, respectively, then the minimum percentage error is obtained by student no          .
solution:
T=2πℓg⇒g=4π2ℓT2
Δgg=Δℓℓ+2∆TT

ΔT= leastcountoftime ΔT0 numberofoscillations (n) 
Δgg=Δℓℓ+2ΔT0nT

As Δℓ and ΔT0 are same for all observations so
Δgg is minimum for highest value of ℓ, n and T

⇒ Minimum percentage error in g is for student number 1.

subject: Physics
q: Let <math><mover accent="true"><mrow><mi>A</mi></mrow><mo>→</mo></mover><mo>=</mo><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mover accent="true"><mrow><mi>i</mi></mrow><mo>^</mo></mover><mi> </mi><mo>+</mo><mi> </mi><mover accent="true"><mrow><mi>j</mi></mrow><mo>^</mo></mover></mrow></mfenced><mi> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">a</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">n</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">d</mi><mi> </mi><mover accent="true"><mrow><mi>B</mi></mrow><mo>→</mo></mover><mo>=</mo><mo>(</mo><mn>2</mn><mover accent="true"><mrow><mi>i</mi></mrow><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>-</mo><mi> </mi><mover accent="true"><mrow><mi>j</mi></mrow><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>)</mo></math> . The magnitude of a coplanar vector <math><mover accent="true"><mrow><mi>C</mi></mrow><mo>→</mo></mover></math> such that <math><mover accent="true"><mrow><mi>A</mi></mrow><mo>→</mo></mover><mi> </mi><mo>.</mo><mi> </mi><mover accent="true"><mrow><mi>C</mi></mrow><mo>→</mo></mover><mo>=</mo><mover accent="true"><mrow><mi>B</mi></mrow><mo>→</mo></mover><mo>.</mo><mi> </mi><mover accent="true"><mrow><mi>C</mi></mrow><mo>→</mo></mover><mo>=</mo><mi> </mi><mover accent="true"><mrow><mi>A</mi></mrow><mo>→</mo></mover><mi> </mi><mo>.</mo><mi> </mi><mover accent="true"><mrow><mi>B</mi></mrow><mo>→</mo></mover></math> is given by:
question: Let <math><mover accent="true"><mrow><mi>A</mi></mrow><mo>→</mo></mover><mo>=</mo><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mover accent="true"><mrow><mi>i</mi></mrow><mo>^</mo></mover><mi> </mi><mo>+</mo><mi> </mi><mover accent="true"><mrow><mi>j</mi></mrow><mo>^</mo></mover></mrow></mfenced><mi> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">a</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">n</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">d</mi><mi> </mi><mover accent="true"><mrow><mi>B</mi></mrow><mo>→</mo></mover><mo>=</mo><mo>(</mo><mn>2</mn><mover accent="true"><mrow><mi>i</mi></mrow><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>-</mo><mi> </mi><mover accent="true"><mrow><mi>j</mi></mrow><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>)</mo></math> . The magnitude of a coplanar vector <math><mover accent="true"><mrow><mi>C</mi></mrow><mo>→</mo></mover></math> such that <math><mover accent="true"><mrow><mi>A</mi></mrow><mo>→</mo></mover><mi> </mi><mo>.</mo><mi> </mi><mover accent="true"><mrow><mi>C</mi></mrow><mo>→</mo></mover><mo>=</mo><mover accent="true"><mrow><mi>B</mi></mrow><mo>→</mo></mover><mo>.</mo><mi> </mi><mover accent="true"><mrow><mi>C</mi></mrow><mo>→</mo></mover><mo>=</mo><mi> </mi><mover accent="true"><mrow><mi>A</mi></mrow><mo>→</mo></mover><mi> </mi><mo>.</mo><mi> </mi><mover accent="true"><mrow><mi>B</mi></mrow><mo>→</mo></mover></math> is given by:
solution:
If C→=ai^ + bj^ then, 
A→ . C→=A→ . B→  
a+b=1  .......(i)
B→. C→= A→ . B→
2a-b=1  ......(ii)
solving equations (i) and (ii) we get, 
a=13, b=23
C→= 19+49= 59

q: A physical quantity <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>y</mi></math> is represented by the formula <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>y</mi><mo>=</mo><msup><mi>m</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><msup><mi>r</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>4</mn></mrow></msup><msup><mrow><mo mathvariant="italic"> </mo><mi mathvariant="italic">g</mi></mrow><mi mathvariant="italic">x</mi></msup><msup><mi>l</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mfrac><mn>3</mn><mn>2</mn></mfrac></mrow></msup></math> If the percentage errors found in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>y</mi><mo mathvariant="italic">,</mo><mo mathvariant="italic"> </mo><mi>m</mi><mo mathvariant="italic">,</mo><mo mathvariant="italic"> </mo><mi>r</mi><mo mathvariant="italic">,</mo><mo mathvariant="italic"> </mo><mi>l</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>g</mi></math> are <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>18</mn><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mn>1</mn><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mn>4</mn></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>p</mi></math> respectively, then find the value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>p</mi></math>.

q: Let <math><mfenced close="|" open="|" separators="|"><mrow><mover accent="true"><mrow><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>1</mn></mrow></msub></mrow><mo>→</mo></mover></mrow></mfenced><mo>=</mo><mn>3</mn><mo>,</mo><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mfenced close="|" open="|" separators="|"><mrow><mover accent="true"><mrow><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msub></mrow><mo>→</mo></mover></mrow></mfenced><mo>=</mo><mn>5</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi></math> and <math><mfenced close="|" open="|" separators="|"><mrow><mover accent="true"><mrow><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>1</mn></mrow></msub></mrow><mo>→</mo></mover><mo>+</mo><mover accent="true"><mrow><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msub></mrow><mo>→</mo></mover></mrow></mfenced><mo>=</mo><mn>5</mn><mo>.</mo></math> The value of <math><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mn>2</mn><mover accent="true"><mrow><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>1</mn></mrow></msub></mrow><mo>→</mo></mover><mo>+</mo><mn>3</mn><mover accent="true"><mrow><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msub></mrow><mo>→</mo></mover></mrow></mfenced><mo>∙</mo><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mn>3</mn><mover accent="true"><mrow><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>1</mn></mrow></msub></mrow><mo>→</mo></mover><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn><mover accent="true"><mrow><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msub></mrow><mo>→</mo></mover></mrow></mfenced></math> is:
question: Let <math><mfenced close="|" open="|" separators="|"><mrow><mover accent="true"><mrow><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>1</mn></mrow></msub></mrow><mo>→</mo></mover></mrow></mfenced><mo>=</mo><mn>3</mn><mo>,</mo><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mfenced close="|" open="|" separators="|"><mrow><mover accent="true"><mrow><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msub></mrow><mo>→</mo></mover></mrow></mfenced><mo>=</mo><mn>5</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi></math> and <math><mfenced close="|" open="|" separators="|"><mrow><mover accent="true"><mrow><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>1</mn></mrow></msub></mrow><mo>→</mo></mover><mo>+</mo><mover accent="true"><mrow><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msub></mrow><mo>→</mo></mover></mrow></mfenced><mo>=</mo><mn>5</mn><mo>.</mo></math> The value of <math><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mn>2</mn><mover accent="true"><mrow><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>1</mn></mrow></msub></mrow><mo>→</mo></mover><mo>+</mo><mn>3</mn><mover accent="true"><mrow><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msub></mrow><mo>→</mo></mover></mrow></mfenced><mo>∙</mo><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mn>3</mn><mover accent="true"><mrow><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>1</mn></mrow></msub></mrow><mo>→</mo></mover><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn><mover accent="true"><mrow><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msub></mrow><mo>→</mo></mover></mrow></mfenced></math> is:
solution:
A→1+A→2=5
A→1+A→22=25
A→12+A→22+2A→1A→2=25
9+25+2A→1∙A→2=25
A→1.A→2=-92
Now, 2A→1+3A→2∙3A→1-2A→2=6A→12-4A→1A→2+9A→1A→2-6A→22
=6.A→12+5A→1A→2-6A→22
=6×9+5-92-6×25
=54-452-150
= -118.5

subject: Physics
q: A torque meter is calibrated to reference standards of mass, length and time each with <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mo>%</mo></math> accuracy. After calibration, the measured torque with this torque meter will have net accuracy of
question: A torque meter is calibrated to reference standards of mass, length and time each with <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mo>%</mo></math> accuracy. After calibration, the measured torque with this torque meter will have net accuracy of
solution:
Dimensional formula for Torque

τ=ML2 T-2

Now the percentage error in torque can be calculated by

τ%=1×∆MM+2×∆LL+2×∆TT×100⇒τ%=25%

q:
A particle moves from the point $(2.0 \hat{i}+4.0 \hat{j}) \mathrm{m},$ at $\mathrm{t}=0$,

with an initial velocity $(5.0 \hat{i}+4.0 \hat{j}) \mathrm{ms}^{-1} .$ It is acted upon by a constant force which produces a constant acceleration

$(4.0 \hat{i}+4.0 \hat{j}) \mathrm{ms}^{-2} .$ What is the distance of the particle

from the origin at time $2 \mathrm{~s} ?$

subject: Physics
q: A person moved from A to B on a circular path as shown in figure. If the distance travelled by him is 60 m, then the magnitude of displacement would be : (Given cos135°=-0.7)
question: A person moved from A to B on a circular path as shown in figure. If the distance travelled by him is 60 m, then the magnitude of displacement would be : (Given cos135°=-0.7)
solution:
The distance travelled by the person d will be equal to the arch length.

⇒d=Rθ

⇒60=R3π4

⇒R=60×43π=80πm

Now the magnitude of displacement can be found using subtraction of vectors. Therefore,

s=R22+R12-2R1R2cosθ⇒s=R2+R2-2R2cos135°

⇒2R2-2R2-0.7

⇒3.4R2=3.480π2

⇒R=47 m

question: The magnitude of vectors OA→, OB→ and OC→ in the given figure are equal. The direction of OA→+OB→-OC→ with x-axis will be:
solution:
From the above diagram, the equations are, 
OA→=Rcos30∘i^+sin30∘j^

OB→=Rcos60∘i^-sin60°j^

OC→=R-cos45°i^+sin45°j^

⇒OA→+OB→−OC→=R12+32+12i^+R12−32−12j^, angle with the x-axis.

⇒tanα=12−32−1212+32+12=1−3−21+3+2

⇒α=tan−11−3−21+3+2.

question: A body is moving with constant speed, in a circle of radius <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math>. solution:
Give: r=10 m, T=4 s.

Displacement is defined to be the change in position of an object. As we can see from the above figure, displacement will be d=r2+r2

⇒d=102 m

subject: Physics
q: Three forces F1=10 N, F2=8 N, F3=6 N are acting on a particle of mass 5 kg. The forces F2 and F3 are applied perpendicularly so that particle remains at rest. If the force F1 is removed, then the acceleration of the particle is
question: Three forces F1=10 N, F2=8 N, F3=6 N are acting on a particle of mass 5 kg. The forces F2 and F3 are applied perpendicularly so that particle remains at rest. If the force F1 is removed, then the acceleration of the particle is
solution:
When the force F1 is removed, the particle starts accelerating because of the resultant force between F2 and F3.

The resultant force can be calculated as follows:

F=F22+F32=10 N

Hence, the acceleration of the particle is given by

a=10 N5 kg=2 m s–2

subject: Physics
q: In an experiment to find acceleration due to gravity <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mi>g</mi></mfenced></math> using simple pendulum, time period of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></math> is measured from time of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>100</mn></math> oscillation with a watch of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></math> resolution. If measured value of length is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> known to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mm</mi></math> accuracy. The accuracy in the determination of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>g</mi></math> is found to be <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo>%</mo></math>. The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> is
question: In an experiment to find acceleration due to gravity <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mi>g</mi></mfenced></math> using simple pendulum, time period of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></math> is measured from time of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>100</mn></math> oscillation with a watch of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></math> resolution. If measured value of length is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> known to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mm</mi></math> accuracy. The accuracy in the determination of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>g</mi></math> is found to be <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo>%</mo></math>. The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> is
solution:
Time period of oscillation is given by T=2πlg , here, l is length and g is acceleration due to gravity.

From above relation, we have  g=14π2T2l.

Fractional error in g is stated as 

Δgg=2ΔTT+Δll

⇒Δgg×100=2×1100×0.5×100+1 mm10 cm×100

⇒Δgg×100=5100×100=5%

Hence, value of x=5.

q:
A particle is moving with a velocity v→=Kyi^+xj^, where K is a constant.

The general equation for its path is:
question:
A particle is moving with a velocity v→=Kyi^+xj^, where K is a constant.

The general equation for its path is:
options: <math><msup><mrow><mi>y</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup><mo>=</mo><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">c</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">o</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">n</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">t</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">a</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">n</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">t</mi></math>

<math><mi>x</mi><mi>y</mi><mo>=</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">c</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">o</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">n</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">t</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">a</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">n</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">t</mi></math>

<math><mi>y</mi><mo>=</mo><msup><mrow><mi>x</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup><mo>+</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">c</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">o</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">n</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">t</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">a</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">n</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">t</mi></math>

<math><msup><mrow><mi>y</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup><mo>=</mo><msup><mrow><mi>x</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup><mo>+</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">c</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">o</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">n</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">t</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">a</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">n</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">t</mi></math>
solution:
v→=Kyi^+xj^

vx=y=dxdt

vy=x=dydt

dxdy=yx

∫x dx=∫y dy

x22=y22+C

or y2=x2+constant

q: A silver wire has a mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>6</mn><mo>±</mo><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>006</mn></mrow></mfenced><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math>, radius <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn><mo>±</mo><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>005</mn></mrow></mfenced><mi>mm</mi></math> and length <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mn>4</mn><mo>±</mo><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>04</mn></mrow></mfenced><mi>cm</mi></math>. The maximum percentage error in the measurement of its density will be
question: A silver wire has a mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>6</mn><mo>±</mo><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>006</mn></mrow></mfenced><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math>, radius <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn><mo>±</mo><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>005</mn></mrow></mfenced><mi>mm</mi></math> and length <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mn>4</mn><mo>±</mo><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>04</mn></mrow></mfenced><mi>cm</mi></math>. The maximum percentage error in the measurement of its density will be
solution:
Volume of the wire is V=πr2l.

Density of the wire is ρ=mπr2l

Relative error in measurement of density

⇒Δρρ=Δmm+2×Δrr+Δll

=0.0060.6+2×0.0050.5+0.044

=0.01+0.02+0.01=0.04

∴ Percentage error in ρ=0.04×100=4%

subject: Physics
q: The area of a square is <math><mn>5.29</mn><mi> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">c</mi><msup><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup><mo>.</mo></math> The area of <math><mn>7</mn></math> such squares taking into account the significant figures is:
question: The area of a square is <math><mn>5.29</mn><mi> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">c</mi><msup><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup><mo>.</mo></math> The area of <math><mn>7</mn></math> such squares taking into account the significant figures is:
solution:
Given,
Area of square =5.29 cm2
Area of 7 square =5.29+5.29+5.29+5.29+5.29+5.29+5.29=37.03 cm2
{5.29 has 2 significant digits after decimal. Accordingly, the answer should also have two significant digits after decimal}.

subject: Physics
q: Time periods of oscillation of the same simple pendulum measured using four different measuring clocks were recorded as $4.62 \mathrm{~s}, 4.632 \mathrm{~s}, 4.6 \mathrm{~s}$ and $4.64 \mathrm{~s}$. The arithmetic mean of these readings in correct significant figure is :
question: Time periods of oscillation of the same simple pendulum measured using four different measuring clocks were recorded as $4.62 \mathrm{~s}, 4.632 \mathrm{~s}, 4.6 \mathrm{~s}$ and $4.64 \mathrm{~s}$. The arithmetic mean of these readings in correct significant figure is :
solution:
Sum of number by considering significant digi
$\text {sum }=4.6+4.6+4.6+4.6=18.4$
Arithmetic Mean $=\frac{\text { sum }}{4}=\frac{18.4}{4}=4.6$

subject: Physics
q:
The following observations were taken for determining surface tension T of water by capillary method:

diameter of capillary, D=1.25×10-2 m

rise of water, h=1.45×10-2 m

Using g=9.80 m s-2 and the simplified relation T=rhg2×103 N m-1 the possible error in surface tension is closest to:
question:
The following observations were taken for determining surface tension T of water by capillary method:

diameter of capillary, D=1.25×10-2 m

rise of water, h=1.45×10-2 m

Using g=9.80 m s-2 and the simplified relation T=rhg2×103 N m-1 the possible error in surface tension is closest to:
solution:
Percentage error in surface tension 

∆TT=∆rr+∆hh

∆rr=∆DD=0.011.25

∆hh=0.011.45

∴% error in T=∆TT×100=0.011.25+0.011.45×100=1.489≈1.5
